one_liner: Managed encryption key service for creating, controlling, and rotating cryptographic keys used to encrypt data across AWS services.
when_to_use: Encrypt data at rest in S3, EBS, RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, EFS, and other AWS services
Customer-managed keys (CMKs) when you need control over key policies, rotation, and audit trails
Automatic annual key rotation for compliance requirements
Envelope encryption: encrypt data keys with a KMS key, then use data keys to encrypt data locally
Encrypt secrets, credentials, and sensitive configuration data
when_not_to_use: Need to manage keys on-premises or in an HSM you fully control (use CloudHSM)
Simple encryption where AWS-managed keys are sufficient (SSE-S3 is simpler and free)
Client-side encryption where keys must never enter AWS (use your own key management)
key_facts: Key types: AWS-managed keys (created automatically by services, free), Customer-managed keys (you create and control, $1/month), AWS-owned keys (internal to AWS services, no visibility or cost).
Customer-managed keys: full control over key policy (who can use/manage), deletion scheduling (7-30 day waiting period), and CloudTrail audit logging of all key usage.
Automatic key rotation: enabled per key. Rotates the backing key material annually. Old material is retained for decryption of previously encrypted data. Only for symmetric keys.

Envelope encryption: KMS generates a data key, returns plaintext + encrypted versions. Application encrypts data with plaintext key, stores encrypted key alongside data. Decrypt by calling KMS to decrypt the data key.
Multi-Region keys: replicate keys across regions for cross-region encryption/decryption without cross-region API calls. Useful for DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 cross-region replication.
Key policies + IAM policies together control access. Key policy is the primary access control mechanism for KMS keys.
Grants: temporary, fine-grained permissions for a key without modifying key policy or IAM policy. Used by AWS services internally.
KMS keys never leave KMS unencrypted. All cryptographic operations happen within KMS HSMs (FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated).
API call limits: varies by region (5,500-30,000 requests/second for symmetric operations). Can request increases.
Integrates natively with 80+ AWS services for encryption at rest.
pricing_summary: Customer-managed keys: $1/month per key. API calls: $0.03/10,000 requests. AWS-managed keys: free (but API calls still apply). Multi-Region keys: $1/month per replica.
common_misconceptions: KMS does NOT store your data. It stores and manages encryption keys. Data is encrypted by the service or application using keys from KMS.
SSE-KMS is NOT free. Customer-managed KMS keys cost $1/month and KMS API calls cost $0.03/10K. For cost-sensitive workloads, SSE-S3 (AWS-managed) is free.
KMS automatic rotation does NOT re-encrypt existing data. It only rotates the key material used for NEW encryptions. Old ciphertext is still decryptable using retained old key material.
Deleting a KMS key is NOT immediate. There is a mandatory 7-30 day waiting period to prevent accidental data loss. Once deleted, all data encrypted with that key becomes unrecoverable.
AWS-managed keys and customer-managed keys are DIFFERENT. AWS-managed keys cannot be rotated on custom schedules, cannot have custom key policies, and cannot be shared cross-account.

one_liner: Managed service for storing, retrieving, and automatically rotating secrets such as database credentials, API keys, and tokens.
when_to_use: Store and manage database credentials with automatic rotation (RDS, Aurora, Redshift, DocumentDB)
Replace hard-coded credentials in application code or configuration files
Automatic credential rotation on a schedule without application downtime
Cross-account secret sharing via resource-based policies
Multi-Region secret replication for disaster recovery and low-latency access
when_not_to_use: Simple configuration values that are not sensitive (use SSM Parameter Store free tier)
Large binary secrets or certificates (use ACM for SSL/TLS certificates)
key_facts: Automatic rotation: built-in rotation Lambda functions for RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB), Aurora, Redshift, and DocumentDB. Custom rotation functions for other secret types.
Rotation uses a Lambda function that updates the secret AND the database password in a single atomic operation. Applications get the new credential on the next retrieval.
Multi-Region replication: replicate secrets across AWS regions. Replicas are read-only and stay in sync automatically. Useful for multi-region applications and DR.
Versioning: secrets have staging labels (AWSCURRENT, AWSPREVIOUS, AWSPENDING) to support seamless rotation without application downtime.
Resource-based policies: share secrets cross-account without creating IAM roles in the target account.
Integration: SDKs and CLI for retrieval. Caching clients available to reduce API calls and latency.
Encryption: all secrets encrypted at rest with KMS (AWS-managed or customer-managed key).
CloudTrail logging: all API calls (GetSecretValue, RotateSecret, etc.) are logged for audit.
Secrets Manager vs. SSM Parameter Store: Secrets Manager has native rotation, costs $0.40/secret/month. Parameter Store SecureString is cheaper ($0.05/10K API calls) but has no built-in rotation.
pricing_summary: Per secret: $0.40/month per secret stored. API calls: $0.05/10,000 API calls. Rotation Lambda: standard Lambda pricing. No charge for replication.
common_misconceptions: Secrets Manager is NOT the same as SSM Parameter Store. Secrets Manager has built-in automatic rotation and native database credential management. Parameter Store is cheaper but requires custom rotation logic.
Automatic rotation does NOT break applications IF you retrieve credentials on each use (not caching indefinitely). The SDK caching client handles rotation transparently.
Multi-Region replication keeps secrets in sync AUTOMATICALLY. You do NOT need to manually update secrets in each region.
Rotation does NOT require a maintenance window. The multi-user rotation strategy creates a new set of credentials while keeping the old ones active until the switch is complete.

one_liner: Continuously records and evaluates AWS resource configurations against desired rules for compliance auditing, change tracking, and security analysis.
when_to_use: Track configuration changes to AWS resources over time (who changed what, when)
Compliance auditing: evaluate resources against rules (e.g., all S3 buckets must be encrypted, all EC2 instances must have tags)
Detect non-compliant resources and trigger automatic or manual remediation
Configuration history: view the timeline of changes for any resource
Multi-account/multi-region compliance with AWS Organizations integration (Config Aggregator)
when_not_to_use: Real-time API call logging and forensics (use CloudTrail)
Cost optimization and billing analysis (use Cost Explorer, Budgets)
key_facts: Configuration recorder: continuously tracks configuration changes for supported AWS resources. Records the current state and all changes over time.

Config rules: evaluate whether resource configurations comply with your policies. AWS provides 300+ managed rules (e.g., s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled, required-tags, rds-multi-az-support).
Custom rules: write your own compliance checks using Lambda functions or Guard policies.
Conformance packs: group related Config rules into a single deployable unit for organizational compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, CIS Benchmarks).
Remediation: automatically fix non-compliant resources using SSM Automation documents. Can be automatic or require manual approval.
Config Aggregator: aggregate compliance data from multiple accounts and regions into a single view. Works with AWS Organizations.
Configuration timeline: view the history of configuration changes for any resource, including relationships between resources.
Delivery channel: config snapshots and change notifications can be delivered to S3 and SNS.
Resource relationships: Config tracks dependencies between resources (e.g., which security group is attached to which EC2 instance).
Supports 200+ AWS resource types including EC2, S3, RDS, IAM, VPC, Lambda, and more.
pricing_summary: Configuration items recorded: $0.003 per item. Config rule evaluations: $0.001 per evaluation. Conformance pack evaluations: $0.001 per evaluation. Storage in S3 for configuration history: standard S3 pricing.
common_misconceptions: AWS Config is NOT CloudTrail. Config records the STATE of resources over time (configuration snapshots). CloudTrail records API CALLS (who did what). They are complementary.
Config rules do NOT prevent non-compliant changes. They DETECT and REPORT non-compliance. Use SCPs or IAM policies to prevent changes, and Config for detection and remediation.
Config is NOT free. It charges per configuration item recorded and per rule evaluation. For large accounts with many resources, costs can add up.
Managed rules cover most common compliance scenarios. You do NOT need to write custom Lambda rules for standard checks like encryption, tagging, or multi-AZ.

one_liner: Records all API calls and account activity across AWS services for governance, compliance auditing, operational troubleshooting, and security forensics.
when_to_use: Audit who made what API call, when, and from where across all AWS services
Security investigation and forensics (detect unauthorized access, compromised credentials)
Compliance requirements for recording all AWS activity (SOX, PCI, HIPAA)
Trigger automated responses to specific API events (via EventBridge integration)
Operational troubleshooting: track resource changes to understand what happened
when_not_to_use: Track resource configuration state over time (use AWS Config)
Performance metrics and alarms (use CloudWatch Metrics)
key_facts: Management events: logged by default for free (90-day history in CloudTrail Event History). Includes control plane operations like CreateBucket, RunInstances, CreateUser.
Data events: high-volume operations on data (S3 object-level operations, Lambda invocations, DynamoDB item-level operations). Must be explicitly enabled. Charged per event.
Trails: deliver events to S3 bucket and optionally to CloudWatch Logs. Can be single-region or multi-region (recommended). Organization trails cover all accounts.
CloudTrail Lake: managed data lake for querying CloudTrail events with SQL. Replaces the need to set up Athena queries on S3-stored logs.
Event history: free, 90-day view of management events in the AWS Console. No trail required.
CloudTrail Insights: automatically detects unusual API activity patterns (e.g., spike in RunInstances calls). Additional charge.
Integration with EventBridge: create rules to trigger actions based on specific API calls (e.g., alert when someone creates an IAM user, trigger remediation when a security group is modified).
Log file integrity validation: SHA-256 hash digest files verify that log files have not been modified or deleted.
Multi-account: organization trail in the management account captures events from all member accounts.
S3 log delivery: logs are delivered within 15 minutes of the API call. Not real-time.
pricing_summary: Management events: first copy free, $2/100K events for additional copies. Data events: $0.10/100K events. CloudTrail Lake: $2.50/GB ingested, $0.005/GB scanned. Insights: $0.35/100K events analyzed.
common_misconceptions: CloudTrail is NOT the same as AWS Config. CloudTrail logs API CALLS (actions). Config records resource STATES (configurations). Use both together for complete governance.
CloudTrail Event History provides 90 days of management events for FREE without creating a trail. You do NOT need to set up a trail for basic auditing.
Data events (S3 GetObject, PutObject) are NOT logged by default. You must explicitly enable them in a trail. This is a common exam question.
CloudTrail logs are NOT delivered in real-time. There is a delay of up to 15 minutes. For real-time event processing, use EventBridge with CloudTrail integration.
Organization trails automatically capture events from ALL member accounts. You do NOT need to configure trails in each individual account.
